title: DBMS
purpose: Understand database architecture, relational theory, transactions and query execution.
outcomes: Explain DBMS concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important DBMS features
Implement, test and debug practical DBMS solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of DBMS before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Data models and schemas
topics.definition: Data models and schemas is a fundamentals part of DBMS concerned with how engineers store, retrieve and transform information with explicit correctness and performance guarantees. It provides a vocabulary, operating model and constraints for making that part of a system deliberate rather than accidental.
topics.whyItMatters: Without understanding Data models and schemas, a DBMS solution may work on the happy path but become difficult to change, debug or operate. This topic makes ownership, behavior and failure conditions explicit.
whenToUse: Use Data models and schemas when it directly supports a required DBMS behavior
Use it after identifying the input, output, constraints and failure policy
Use it when the team can test and operate the resulting behavior
whenNotToUse: Do not add Data models and schemas only because it is popular or appears in an architecture diagram
Avoid it when its consistency, query or operational model conflicts with the access pattern
Avoid abstraction before the repeated problem and ownership boundary are understood
internalWorking: Input enters the Data models and schemas boundary and is validated against its contract
Data is encoded into storage structures and accessed through planned read and write paths
State changes should preserve a named invariant and produce an observable result
Failures propagate through a defined error, retry or recovery path
tradeoffs: Correctness and clarity versus implementation effort
Runtime performance versus memory or operational complexity
Flexibility versus a smaller, easier-to-test contract
commonMistakes: Memorizing the definition of Data models and schemas without tracing an example
Ignoring invalid input, failure and cleanup behavior
Choosing an advanced implementation before measuring the simpler one
topics.practicalExample: In the placement platform, apply Data models and schemas to one DBMS workflow such as creating an assessment, loading study content or calculating results. Define the contract, implement the happy path, reject invalid input and record enough context to diagnose failure.

topics.title: Relational algebra
topics.definition: SQL is a declarative language for defining, querying and changing relational data while the database optimizer chooses an execution plan.
topics.whyItMatters: It expresses data relationships, constraints and set-based transformations close to durable storage.
whenToUse: Model data with strong relationships and transactional invariants
Join, aggregate and rank related records
Let constraints reject invalid data regardless of application path
whenNotToUse: Fetching all rows to filter in application memory
Using SELECT * for stable production contracts
Adding indexes without understanding read and write paths
internalWorking: Parsing and binding resolve names and types
The optimizer estimates row counts and compares access paths
The executor scans indexes or tables and applies joins, filters and aggregates
Transactions coordinate visibility and durability through locks or multiversion mechanisms
tradeoffs: Normalization protects consistency but may require joins
Indexes accelerate selected reads but consume space and slow writes
Stronger isolation prevents anomalies but can reduce concurrency
commonMistakes: Filtering a LEFT JOINed table in WHERE and accidentally making it inner
Ignoring NULL three-valued logic
Paginating large changing data with deep OFFSET
topics.practicalExample: Group students by section, calculate placement rate with conditional aggregation, and index the foreign key used to join students to sections.

topics.title: Storage and pages
topics.definition: An AI application combines a probabilistic model with deterministic context, tools, policies and evaluation. Retrieval supplies evidence; tool calls perform bounded actions; orchestration controls state and flow.
topics.whyItMatters: A language model alone may produce fluent but unsupported output and cannot safely access current private data or take actions without explicit controls.
whenToUse: Use prompting for instruction and output shaping
Use RAG when answers must be grounded in changing documents
Use tools or agents only when the task needs external information or action
whenNotToUse: Using an autonomous loop for a deterministic workflow
Treating retrieved text as trusted instructions
Shipping without a representative evaluation set
internalWorking: Input is tokenized and the model predicts successive token distributions
Retrieval embeds or searches a query and returns evidence chunks
An orchestrator builds context, invokes the model, validates output and optionally executes approved tools
Traces and evaluators measure quality, safety, latency and cost
tradeoffs: More context may improve recall but increase noise, latency and cost
Agents are flexible but less predictable than workflows
Stricter output validation improves reliability but needs fallback handling
commonMistakes: Evaluating only a few happy-path examples
Giving tools excessive permission
Confusing RAG with model training
topics.practicalExample: Retrieve authorized study chapters, rerank them, ask the model to answer with citations, reject unsupported claims and route low-confidence answers to a normal search or human review path.

topics.title: Transactions and ACID
topics.definition: A transaction groups database operations into one logical unit with atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability guarantees.
topics.whyItMatters: Concurrent requests and failures must not expose half-finished updates or violate business invariants.
whenToUse: Multiple writes must succeed or fail together
Protect invariants such as one submission per attempt
Coordinate reads and writes that require a stable view
whenNotToUse: Holding a transaction open across network calls or user input
Using the strongest isolation without understanding contention
Assuming transactions automatically span unrelated services
internalWorking: The engine records changes in a log before durable data pages
Locks or MVCC control which versions concurrent transactions can observe
Commit makes the unit durable and visible according to isolation
Rollback discards uncommitted effects
tradeoffs: Stronger isolation prevents more anomalies but may block or abort more work
Long transactions preserve a view but retain locks or old versions
Distributed transactions offer coordination at high availability and latency cost
commonMistakes: Doing HTTP calls inside a transaction
Not retrying serialization or deadlock victims
Reading then writing without protecting the invariant
topics.practicalExample: Create an assessment attempt and reserve its unique student-assessment key in one transaction, then publish a notification only after commit.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Transactions and ACID.
interviewQuestions.answer: A transaction is an atomic unit whose changes become durable at commit. Isolation is implemented with locking, MVCC or both and determines which concurrency anomalies are possible. I keep transactions short, protect concrete invariants and handle deadlock or serialization retries.

topics.title: Concurrency control
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Confusing concurrency with parallelism
Launching unbounded work
Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.

topics.title: Indexes
topics.definition: A database index is an auxiliary ordered or hashed structure that finds rows without scanning the entire table.
topics.whyItMatters: Large tables make full scans expensive when queries repeatedly filter, join or order by selective columns.
whenToUse: Columns used in selective filters and joins
Composite access paths matching query prefix and order
Covering frequently executed reads after measuring plans
whenNotToUse: Indexing every column
Low-selectivity indexes that the optimizer cannot use profitably
Duplicate indexes whose leading columns provide the same access path
internalWorking: A B+ tree keeps sorted keys in internal and leaf pages
Traversal follows key ranges to leaf entries that identify rows
Composite indexes are ordered lexicographically, so leading-column order matters
Writes must update each relevant index and may split pages
tradeoffs: Faster reads versus slower writes and more storage
Wide covering indexes avoid lookups but increase maintenance
Indexes improve one access pattern and may not help another
commonMistakes: Assuming an index is used without checking EXPLAIN
Applying a function that makes a predicate non-sargable
Using the wrong column order in a composite index
topics.practicalExample: For WHERE section_id = ? AND status = ? ORDER BY score DESC, evaluate an index beginning with section_id and status, then score, and confirm it with the actual query plan.

topics.title: Transactions and ACID: applied patterns
topics.definition: A transaction groups database operations into one logical unit with atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability guarantees.
topics.whyItMatters: Concurrent requests and failures must not expose half-finished updates or violate business invariants.
whenToUse: Multiple writes must succeed or fail together
Protect invariants such as one submission per attempt
Coordinate reads and writes that require a stable view
whenNotToUse: Holding a transaction open across network calls or user input
Using the strongest isolation without understanding contention
Assuming transactions automatically span unrelated services
internalWorking: The engine records changes in a log before durable data pages
Locks or MVCC control which versions concurrent transactions can observe
Commit makes the unit durable and visible according to isolation
Rollback discards uncommitted effects
tradeoffs: Stronger isolation prevents more anomalies but may block or abort more work
Long transactions preserve a view but retain locks or old versions
Distributed transactions offer coordination at high availability and latency cost
commonMistakes: Doing HTTP calls inside a transaction
Not retrying serialization or deadlock victims
Reading then writing without protecting the invariant
topics.practicalExample: Create an assessment attempt and reserve its unique student-assessment key in one transaction, then publish a notification only after commit.

topics.title: Concurrency control: applied patterns
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Confusing concurrency with parallelism
Launching unbounded work
Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.

topics.title: Indexes: applied patterns
topics.definition: A database index is an auxiliary ordered or hashed structure that finds rows without scanning the entire table.
topics.whyItMatters: Large tables make full scans expensive when queries repeatedly filter, join or order by selective columns.
whenToUse: Columns used in selective filters and joins
Composite access paths matching query prefix and order
Covering frequently executed reads after measuring plans
whenNotToUse: Indexing every column
Low-selectivity indexes that the optimizer cannot use profitably
Duplicate indexes whose leading columns provide the same access path
internalWorking: A B+ tree keeps sorted keys in internal and leaf pages
Traversal follows key ranges to leaf entries that identify rows
Composite indexes are ordered lexicographically, so leading-column order matters
Writes must update each relevant index and may split pages
tradeoffs: Faster reads versus slower writes and more storage
Wide covering indexes avoid lookups but increase maintenance
Indexes improve one access pattern and may not help another
commonMistakes: Assuming an index is used without checking EXPLAIN
Applying a function that makes a predicate non-sargable
Using the wrong column order in a composite index
topics.practicalExample: For WHERE section_id = ? AND status = ? ORDER BY score DESC, evaluate an index beginning with section_id and status, then score, and confirm it with the actual query plan.

topics.title: Partitioning
topics.definition: System design turns functional requirements and quality goals into components, data ownership, interfaces and failure policies.
topics.whyItMatters: A correct single-process feature may fail under traffic, data growth, dependency failure, security constraints or operational limits.
whenToUse: Requirements include scale, reliability or cross-service coordination
A design must expose explicit data and failure ownership
Interview questions ask for reasoned tradeoffs rather than one perfect architecture
whenNotToUse: Starting with microservices before defining requirements
Adding cache or queues without a concrete bottleneck
Claiming high availability without a failure and recovery model
internalWorking: Estimate traffic, storage and latency budgets
Define APIs and the source of truth
Design a simple end-to-end request path
Scale measured bottlenecks and add resilience, observability and security
tradeoffs: Consistency versus availability during partitions
Synchronous simplicity versus asynchronous resilience
Operational complexity versus independent scale
commonMistakes: Skipping capacity estimates
Naming technologies without explaining purpose
Ignoring retries, idempotency and data migration

topics.title: Backup and restore
topics.definition: A REST-style API models addressable resources over HTTP and uses standard methods, status codes, headers and representations to communicate state transitions.
topics.whyItMatters: Clients and services need a stable, understandable contract that can evolve independently.
whenToUse: Resource-oriented request-response APIs
Operations that fit HTTP caching and idempotency semantics
Public or cross-team contracts that benefit from broad tooling
whenNotToUse: Forcing every domain command into awkward CRUD
Using GET for state changes
Returning 200 for every success and failure
internalWorking: A client resolves DNS, creates a secure connection and sends an HTTP request
Routing selects a handler that authenticates, validates and calls domain logic
The service reads or changes data and serializes a representation
Intermediaries may cache or retry only when method semantics allow it
tradeoffs: Simple interoperable contracts versus over-fetching or multiple round trips
Stateless services scale easily but each request needs sufficient context
Versioning protects clients but increases maintenance
commonMistakes: Confusing PUT and PATCH
Non-idempotent retry behavior
Leaking database entities as permanent API contracts
topics.practicalExample: POST /assessments creates and returns 201 with a Location header; GET retrieves it; PATCH updates selected fields; DELETE returns 204 when removal succeeds.

topics.title: Isolation levels
topics.definition: A transaction groups database operations into one logical unit with atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability guarantees.
topics.whyItMatters: Concurrent requests and failures must not expose half-finished updates or violate business invariants.
whenToUse: Multiple writes must succeed or fail together
Protect invariants such as one submission per attempt
Coordinate reads and writes that require a stable view
whenNotToUse: Holding a transaction open across network calls or user input
Using the strongest isolation without understanding contention
Assuming transactions automatically span unrelated services
internalWorking: The engine records changes in a log before durable data pages
Locks or MVCC control which versions concurrent transactions can observe
Commit makes the unit durable and visible according to isolation
Rollback discards uncommitted effects
tradeoffs: Stronger isolation prevents more anomalies but may block or abort more work
Long transactions preserve a view but retain locks or old versions
Distributed transactions offer coordination at high availability and latency cost
commonMistakes: Doing HTTP calls inside a transaction
Not retrying serialization or deadlock victims
Reading then writing without protecting the invariant
topics.practicalExample: Create an assessment attempt and reserve its unique student-assessment key in one transaction, then publish a notification only after commit.

topics.title: B+ trees
topics.definition: A data structure organizes values so required operations have predictable time and space costs.
topics.whyItMatters: The same data can be represented in ways that make lookup, insertion, ordering or traversal dramatically cheaper or more expensive.
whenToUse: Choose arrays for compact indexed access
Choose hashing for fast average membership or lookup
Choose trees or heaps when order, hierarchy or priority drives operations
whenNotToUse: Selecting from habit rather than operation requirements
Ignoring memory overhead and input limits
Using a sophisticated structure before a simple one fails constraints
internalWorking: Representation determines which locations must be visited for each operation
Contiguous storage improves cache locality
Pointer-based structures allow flexible links but add indirection
Invariants such as heap order or tree balance preserve complexity guarantees
tradeoffs: Time versus space
Fast average behavior versus stronger worst-case guarantees
Simple implementation versus specialized operations
commonMistakes: Quoting complexity without defining the operation
Ignoring amortized cost
Using recursion without considering depth
topics.practicalExample: Use a queue for breadth-first prerequisite traversal, a set for visited skills and a map from skill to dependent roadmap nodes.

topics.title: Relational algebra
topics.definition: SQL is a declarative language for defining, querying and changing relational data while the database optimizer chooses an execution plan.
topics.whyItMatters: It expresses data relationships, constraints and set-based transformations close to durable storage.
whenToUse: Model data with strong relationships and transactional invariants
Join, aggregate and rank related records
Let constraints reject invalid data regardless of application path
whenNotToUse: Fetching all rows to filter in application memory
Using SELECT * for stable production contracts
Adding indexes without understanding read and write paths
internalWorking: Parsing and binding resolve names and types
The optimizer estimates row counts and compares access paths
The executor scans indexes or tables and applies joins, filters and aggregates
Transactions coordinate visibility and durability through locks or multiversion mechanisms
tradeoffs: Normalization protects consistency but may require joins
Indexes accelerate selected reads but consume space and slow writes
Stronger isolation prevents anomalies but can reduce concurrency
commonMistakes: Filtering a LEFT JOINed table in WHERE and accidentally making it inner
Ignoring NULL three-valued logic
Paginating large changing data with deep OFFSET
topics.practicalExample: Group students by section, calculate placement rate with conditional aggregation, and index the foreign key used to join students to sections.

topics.title: Normalization
topics.definition: Normalization is a revision part of DBMS concerned with how engineers store, retrieve and transform information with explicit correctness and performance guarantees. It provides a vocabulary, operating model and constraints for making that part of a system deliberate rather than accidental.
topics.whyItMatters: Without understanding Normalization, a DBMS solution may work on the happy path but become difficult to change, debug or operate. This topic makes ownership, behavior and failure conditions explicit.
whenToUse: Use Normalization when it directly supports a required DBMS behavior
Use it after identifying the input, output, constraints and failure policy
Use it when the team can test and operate the resulting behavior
whenNotToUse: Do not add Normalization only because it is popular or appears in an architecture diagram
Avoid it when its consistency, query or operational model conflicts with the access pattern
Avoid abstraction before the repeated problem and ownership boundary are understood
internalWorking: Input enters the Normalization boundary and is validated against its contract

Failures propagate through a defined error, retry or recovery path
tradeoffs: Correctness and clarity versus implementation effort
Runtime performance versus memory or operational complexity
Flexibility versus a smaller, easier-to-test contract
commonMistakes: Memorizing the definition of Normalization without tracing an example
Ignoring invalid input, failure and cleanup behavior
Choosing an advanced implementation before measuring the simpler one

topics.title: Isolation levels
topics.definition: A transaction groups database operations into one logical unit with atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability guarantees.
topics.whyItMatters: Concurrent requests and failures must not expose half-finished updates or violate business invariants.
whenToUse: Multiple writes must succeed or fail together
Protect invariants such as one submission per attempt
Coordinate reads and writes that require a stable view
whenNotToUse: Holding a transaction open across network calls or user input
Using the strongest isolation without understanding contention
Assuming transactions automatically span unrelated services
internalWorking: The engine records changes in a log before durable data pages
Locks or MVCC control which versions concurrent transactions can observe
Commit makes the unit durable and visible according to isolation
Rollback discards uncommitted effects
tradeoffs: Stronger isolation prevents more anomalies but may block or abort more work
Long transactions preserve a view but retain locks or old versions
Distributed transactions offer coordination at high availability and latency cost
commonMistakes: Doing HTTP calls inside a transaction
Not retrying serialization or deadlock victims
Reading then writing without protecting the invariant
topics.practicalExample: Create an assessment attempt and reserve its unique student-assessment key in one transaction, then publish a notification only after commit.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Isolation levels.
interviewQuestions.answer: A transaction is an atomic unit whose changes become durable at commit. Isolation is implemented with locking, MVCC or both and determines which concurrency anomalies are possible. I keep transactions short, protect concrete invariants and handle deadlock or serialization retries.
